title: Feature Request:  recover from failed pull without restarting
body:
/kind feature

**Description**
When pulling down a an image large layer (>1GB) through Artifactory the download fails often towards the end.  This seems to be a known issue with Docker also, and has lots of questions posted.

When you pull the same image the download of the layer starts all over again, and the same problem occurs, so it may take many attempts to get the image. (And of course consume incredible amounts of bandwidth.)

It would be good to have the partial layer saved, and when the pull is attempted again for the download to resume from where it left off.  (Like most web browsers.)

**Steps to reproduce the issue:**

1.  Create image with very large layers and store in repository

2.  Pull image over slow and possibly unreliable network.

3. Have pull fail when layer is close to complete.

**Describe the results you received:**
4. Pull image again, download for layer restarts from zero.

**Describe the results you expected:**
4. Pull image again, download for layer restarts from point of failure.


**Additional information you deem important (e.g. issue happens only occasionally):**
Happens a lot

**Output of `podman version`:**

podman version 2.0.5

**Output of `podman info --debug`:**

**Have you tested with the latest version of Podman and have you checked the Podman Troubleshooting Guide?**

No

**Additional environment details (AWS, VirtualBox, physical, etc.):**

comments.body:
Workaround for docker?
https://forums.docker.com/t/docker-pull-results-in-request-canceled-while-waiting-for-connection-client-timeout-exceeded-while-awaiting-headers/73064/7
@mtrmac PTAL

Taking advantage of _successfully pulled_ layers on future pulls might happen as a side effect of parallel layer commit Valentin was working on some time ago.

Restarting an _individual layer pull_ with a range request is problematic with the current architecture, where compression/decompression/encryption/decryption happens on the fly, so there may not be any obvious restart point. It seems feasible in principle for the pull path (where we seem to record the original blob — currently at least), but the complexity and API implications are not at all trivial.
@vrothberg FYI
@rhatdan, let's discuss in the next planning when to tackle it. We may well get this in for v3.0. Once Podman v2.1 is out, we have some wiggle room for bigger changes.
I agree this seems like a good 3.0 feature.

If you implement this, a flag to indicate that partial layers are being cached, and therefore worst performance/space, would be acceptable. e.g. --cache-layers, or --cache-layers-dir=<dir> or ....  

I was thinking you could do something like pull the compressed/encrypted file and store it all on disk until it is all copied.  And then use the disk copy as the source instead of the network.  (Wild idea not knowing anything about your code, so the idea might be completely wrong.)
A friendly reminder that this issue had no activity for 30 days.
@vrothberg I think we should move this up the priority list after shortnames is complete.

> @vrothberg I think we should move this up the priority list after shortnames is complete.

Before or after image volumes?
Probably after. :^(
A friendly reminder that this issue had no activity for 30 days.
@vrothberg do you have time to look at this?

> @vrothberg do you have time to look at this?

This would be a by-product of reworking the commit-logic in c/image.  It's a larger work item but it's up to the Jedi council if I have time for it :)
This did not make it into 3.0 release.
@rhatdan I think we should tackle this item after bug week(s). Once this feature here is addressed, we can tackle the image package.
SGTM
A friendly reminder that this issue had no activity for 30 days.
@vrothberg Another on your list to improve pulls.
ACK.  That will actually be fixed with the "commit as early as possible" item I am currently working on.
A friendly reminder that this issue had no activity for 30 days.
This has been released with containers/image v5.11.0 which has been merged into Podman :)

I have podman 3.0.0, where can I test this? I have a timeout due to a big file (can i just increase the timeout) and it retries download everytime, i get to 1.7GB (half the blob) and it retries everytime, eventually resulting in a /tmp out of disk space
3.0 is probably too old.  The new behavior shipped with Podman v3.2 but I suggest directly updating to 4.3 if possible.
